g_sov_intro: Basic Sentence Structure. Korean follows Subject-Object-Verb order.
g_sov_s0: Subject-Object-Verb Order. In Korean, the verb always comes at the end.
g_sov_s0_ex: 저는 밥을 먹어요.. I eat rice.. 저는 한국어를 공부해요.. I study Korean.
g_sov_s0_tip: Tip: Think of it as "I rice eat".
g_sov_s1: Dropping the Subject. When obvious from context, Koreans drop the subject.
g_sov_s1_ex: 밥 먹었어요?. Did you eat?. 괜찮아요.. I am fine.
g_sov_s1_tip: Tip: In casual conversation, dropping the subject is natural.
g_topic_intro: Topic Markers. Topic markers indicate what the sentence is about.
g_topic_s0: How to Use 은/는. Use 은 after a consonant and 는 after a vowel.
g_topic_s0_ex: 저는 학생이에요.. I am a student.. 한국은 아름다워요.. Korea is beautiful.
g_topic_s0_tip: Tip: 은/는 is like saying "As for..." in English.
g_subject_intro: Subject Markers. Subject markers identify who performs the action.
g_subject_s0: How to Use 이/가. Use 이 after a consonant and 가 after a vowel.
g_subject_s0_ex: 비가 와요.. It is raining.. 고양이가 귀여워요.. The cat is cute.
g_subject_s0_tip: Tip: Use 이/가 for new information or emphasis.
g_object_intro: Object Markers. Object markers indicate what receives the action.
g_object_s0: How to Use 을/를. Use 을 after a consonant and 를 after a vowel.
g_object_s0_ex: 저는 커피를 마셔요.. I drink coffee.. 책을 읽어요.. I read a book.
g_present_intro: Present Tense. The polite present tense ending.
g_present_s0: Conjugation Rules. Remove 다 from dictionary form. If last vowel is ㅏ or ㅗ, add 아요. Otherwise 어요. 하다 becomes 해요.
g_present_s0_ex: 가다 → 가요. go → goes. 먹다 → 먹어요. eat → eats. 공부하다 → 공부해요. study → studies
g_present_s0_tip: Tip: This is the most important conjugation pattern!
g_past_intro: Past Tense. How to talk about things that already happened.
g_past_s0: Past Tense Conjugation. Same vowel rules but add 았 or 었 before 어요. 하다 verbs use 했어요.
g_past_s0_ex: 가다 → 갔어요. went. 먹다 → 먹었어요. ate. 공부하다 → 공부했어요. studied
g_future_intro: Future Tense. Express plans and intentions.
g_future_s0: Future Tense Pattern. Add ㄹ 거예요 after a vowel stem, or 을 거예요 after a consonant.
g_future_s0_ex: 가다 → 갈 거예요. will go. 먹다 → 먹을 거예요. will eat. 내일 영화를 볼 거예요.. I will watch a movie tomorrow.
g_negative_intro: Negative Sentences. Two ways to make negative sentences.
g_negative_s0: Short Negation: 안. Place 안 before the verb.
g_negative_s0_ex: 안 먹어요.. Don't eat.. 안 좋아요.. Not good.
g_negative_s1: Long Negation: -지 않다. Add 지 않다 to the verb stem.
g_negative_s1_ex: 먹지 않아요.. Don't eat.. 좋지 않아요.. Not good.
g_negative_s1_tip: Tip: For 하다 verbs: split noun and 하다.
g_want_intro: Want To. Express what you want to do.
g_want_s0: Using -고 싶다. Add 고 싶다 to the verb stem.
g_want_s0_ex: 한국에 가고 싶어요.. I want to go to Korea.. 김치를 먹고 싶어요.. I want to eat kimchi.. 한국어를 배우고 싶어요.. I want to learn Korean.
g_connect_intro: Connecting Sentences. Connect two clauses with and or but.
g_connect_s0: And: -고. Add 고 to join two actions.
g_connect_s0_ex: 밥을 먹고 커피를 마셔요.. I eat rice and drink coffee.
g_connect_s1: But: -지만. Add 지만 to contrast two clauses.
g_connect_s1_ex: 비싸지만 맛있어요.. It's expensive but delicious.. 어렵지만 재미있어요.. It's difficult but fun.
g_location_intro: Location Particles. Indicate location and direction.
g_location_s0: 에 - Direction / Static Location. 에 marks destination or static location.
g_location_s0_ex: 학교에 가요.. I go to school.. 집에 있어요.. I am at home.
g_location_s1: 에서 - Location of Action. 에서 marks where an action takes place.
g_location_s1_ex: 도서관에서 공부해요.. I study at the library.. 카페에서 커피를 마셔요.. I drink coffee at the cafe.
g_location_s1_tip: Tip: Use 에 for to or at with 있다. Use 에서 for action verbs.
g_speech_intro: Speech Levels. Korean has different speech levels based on formality.
g_speech_s0: Three Main Levels. Formal polite, informal polite, and casual.
g_speech_s0_ex: 감사합니다. Thank you, formal polite. 고마워요. Thank you, informal polite. 고마워. Thanks, casual
g_speech_s0_tip: Tip: Use informal polite as your default.
g_speech_s1: When to Use Each Level. Formal polite for business, informal polite for everyday, casual for close friends.
g_speech_s1_ex: 먹습니다. eat, formal polite. 먹어요. eat, informal polite. 먹어. eat, casual
pron_vowels_intro: Vowel Sounds. Master all Korean vowel sounds with correct mouth positions and avoid common English-speaker mistakes.
pron_consonants_intro: Consonant Sounds. The three-way consonant distinction (plain, aspirated, tense) is THE hardest part for English speakers. Master it here.
pron_sound_changes_intro: Sound Change Rules. Korean spelling and pronunciation often differ because of automatic sound change rules. Learn the patterns to decode real spoken Korean.
pron_batchim_intro: Batchim (Final Consonants). Korean syllables can end with a consonant called batchim. Only 7 sounds are possible in final position, no matter what is written.
pron_intonation_intro: Intonation & Rhythm. Korean rhythm is fundamentally different from English. Learn how to sound natural with correct timing and pitch patterns.
pron_natural_speech_intro: Natural Speech Patterns. Sound like a real Korean speaker by mastering contractions, connected speech, filler words, and casual speed patterns.
pron_honorific_intro: Honorific Pronunciation. Formality in Korean is not just about word choice -- it changes how you speak, your speed, your tone, and even individual sounds.
pron_mistakes_intro: Common Pronunciation Mistakes. The specific sounds and habits that give away non-native speakers. Fix these and your Korean pronunciation will improve dramatically.
cult_honorifics_intro: Korean Honorific System. Korean has 7 speech levels that change based on the listener\
cult_honorifics_s0: The 7 Speech Levels. Korean has seven distinct speech levels, from the most formal to the most casual. In daily life, three are used most often: formal polite (하십시오체), informal polite (해요체), and casual (해체). The remaining four are archaic or literary: 하오체 (semi-formal, old-fashioned), 하게체 (familiar, used by elders to younger adults), 해라체 (plain, used in writing/news), and 하소서체 (deferential, royal/religious).
cult_honorifics_s1: When to Use Formal vs Informal. Formal polite (합니다) is for business meetings, presentations, news broadcasts, talking to elders you do not know well, and customer service. Informal polite (해요) is the everyday default for conversations with strangers, acquaintances, coworkers, and shops. Casual (해) is reserved for close friends of the same age, younger siblings, children, and very intimate relationships.
cult_honorifics_s2: Age Hierarchy and Language. Korean society places enormous importance on age. Even a one-year difference can determine who speaks formally and who speaks casually. When Koreans first meet, they often ask age early to establish the proper speech level. The older person may speak casually while the younger person must use polite or formal speech.
cult_honorifics_s3: The 선배/후배 System. In school and work, seniority creates a strict hierarchy. 선배 (sunbae) refers to someone who entered before you, and 후배 (hubae) refers to someone who entered after you. A 선배 gets respect regardless of actual age. This system shapes everything from who pours drinks to who speaks first.
cult_honorifics_s4: Honorific Verb Endings and Vocabulary. Korean has special honorific vocabulary used when talking about or to someone of higher status. The subject honorific suffix -(으)시 is added to verb stems when the subject is someone you respect. Some verbs have entirely separate honorific forms.
cult_age_intro: Korean Age System. Korea traditionally uses a unique age-counting system where everyone is 1 at birth and gains a year every January 1st. Understanding this is key to Korean social dynamics.
cult_age_s0: Korean Age vs International Age. In traditional Korean age, you are 1 year old at birth and gain a year every January 1st, not on your birthday. A baby born on December 31st turns 2 the very next day. In 2023, South Korea officially adopted international age for legal and administrative purposes, but Korean age is still widely used in daily conversation and social settings.
cult_age_s1: Why Age Matters So Much. Age determines almost everything in Korean social interaction: speech level, who pours drinks, seating order, who pays, and relationship titles. Koreans ask age early in a new relationship not out of rudeness but to establish the social framework for communication.
cult_age_s2: Asking Age Politely. There are different ways to ask someone\
cult_age_s3: Same Age Culture. When two people discover they are the same age (동갑), it is a bonding moment. Being 동갑 means you can speak casually to each other without formality barriers. Koreans often get excited finding a 동갑 friend because it allows a more relaxed relationship.
cult_food_intro: Ordering Food in Korean. Korean food culture is a huge part of daily life. Knowing how to order, ask about dishes, and pay is essential for any visitor or learner.
cult_food_s0: Getting Attention and Ordering. In Korean restaurants, you call the server by saying 여기요 (here!) or 저기요 (excuse me). There is no need to wait to be approached. Pressing a call button on the table is also common. When ready to order, say 주문할게요 (I would like to order).
cult_food_s1: Specific Food Ordering Phrases. Korean restaurants often have set meals (정식), sharing dishes, and side dishes (반찬) that come free with your meal. Water and basic sides are usually self-service or refilled for free.
cult_food_s2: Asking About Ingredients and Allergies. If you have dietary restrictions or allergies, these phrases are essential. Korean food frequently uses common allergens like soy, sesame, shellfish, and wheat.
cult_food_s3: Paying the Bill. In Korea, you pay at the register near the exit, not at the table. One person usually pays for the whole group. Splitting the bill is becoming more common among younger people but is still less typical than in Western countries.
cult_shopping_intro: Shopping & Money. From market haggling to modern card payments, learn the essential phrases for shopping in Korea and understanding Korean currency.
cult_shopping_s0: Asking Prices. The most essential shopping phrase is 얼마예요? (How much is it?). In traditional markets, prices may not be marked. In stores, prices are always in Korean Won.
cult_shopping_s1: Bargaining Phrases. Bargaining is acceptable in traditional markets and some tourist shopping areas like Namdaemun or Dongdaemun. It is NOT appropriate in regular stores, malls, or convenience stores.
cult_shopping_s2: Korean Won Denominations. Korean Won comes in coins of 10, 50, 100, and 500 won, and bills of 1,000, 5,000, 10,000, and 50,000 won. Prices are always in whole won with no decimal points. Common reference: 1,000 won is roughly 0.75 USD.
cult_shopping_s3: Payment Phrases. Card payment is dominant in Korea. Most people use credit cards, debit cards, or mobile payment apps like Samsung Pay or KakaoPay. Knowing these phrases helps with smooth transactions.
cult_business_intro: Business Korean. Korean business culture is highly formal with strict hierarchical etiquette. Proper language and behavior in professional settings are crucial for success.
cult_business_s0: Formal Greetings and Introductions. Business introductions in Korea always involve exchanging business cards with both hands. Bow slightly when greeting. Use the person\
cult_business_s1: Email Phrases. Korean business emails follow a strict format. Start with a greeting, state your purpose clearly, and end with a formal closing. Use formal polite language (합니다 style) throughout.
cult_business_s2: Phone Etiquette. When answering business calls, state your company and name immediately. Speak slowly and clearly in formal polite language. Saying 여보세요 alone is only for personal calls.
cult_business_s3: Common Business Expressions. Korean workplaces have their own set of frequently used expressions for meetings, deadlines, and daily interactions.
cult_business_s4: Company Dinner Culture. 회식 is the tradition of company dinners and after-work drinking. It is a key part of Korean work culture used for team bonding. Attendance is strongly expected. The boss typically pays, and there are specific drinking etiquette rules to follow.
cult_traditions_intro: Korean Traditions. Korea has rich traditions tied to holidays, celebrations, and daily customs. Understanding these adds depth to your language learning and cultural awareness.
cult_traditions_s0: Lunar New Year. 설날 is one of Korea\
cult_traditions_s1: Chuseok (Harvest Festival). 추석 falls on the 15th day of the 8th lunar month (usually September or October). It is a harvest festival similar to Thanksgiving. Families visit ancestral graves, perform 차례 (ancestral rites), eat 송편 (half-moon rice cakes), and celebrate the harvest.
cult_traditions_s2: Birthday Celebrations. Korean birthdays traditionally involve eating 미역국 (seaweed soup), which mothers eat after giving birth for nutrition. Eating it on your birthday honors your mother. Modern celebrations also include cake and candles.
cult_traditions_s3: 100 Days and First Birthday. The 100th day after birth (백일) and the first birthday (돌) are significant celebrations in Korean culture. Historically, infant mortality was high, so surviving 100 days and one year were major milestones. At 돌, a baby is placed before objects (yarn, money, pencil, rice) to predict their future based on what they grab first.
cult_traditions_s4: Respect Customs. Korea has specific physical customs that show respect. Bowing is the primary greeting. When giving or receiving objects, especially to elders, use both hands. When pouring or receiving drinks, support your arm with your other hand.
cult_kdrama_intro: K-Drama & K-Pop Vocabulary. Korean entertainment has its own rich vocabulary. Understanding these common drama phrases and K-pop terms will enhance your viewing experience and fan interactions.
cult_kdrama_s0: Common Drama Phrases and Exclamations. If you watch K-dramas, you will hear these phrases in almost every episode. They are emotional expressions used in dramatic moments, arguments, and everyday conversations.
cult_kdrama_s1: Relationship Terms from Dramas. K-dramas are full of relationship vocabulary. These terms describe the complex web of Korean social and romantic relationships that drive every storyline.
cult_kdrama_s2: K-Pop Fan Vocabulary. K-pop fandom has developed its own extensive vocabulary mixing Korean and English. These terms are used by fans worldwide.
cult_kdrama_s3: Social Media Korean. Korean internet and social media language is heavily abbreviated and uses unique slang. Knowing these helps you understand comments on Korean social media.
cult_survival_intro: Survival Korean. Essential phrases for navigating real-life situations in Korea, from emergencies to public transportation. These phrases can be lifesavers.
cult_survival_s0: Emergency Phrases. In an emergency in Korea, call 119 for fire/ambulance and 112 for police. Knowing basic emergency phrases can save lives.
cult_survival_s1: Asking for Directions. Korea has excellent public transit and most people are willing to help with directions. These phrases will help you find your way.
cult_survival_s2: At the Hospital. Korean hospitals and clinics are efficient and affordable. Walk-in clinics are common. Knowing medical vocabulary helps communicate symptoms.
cult_survival_s3: At Immigration and the Airport. Arriving in Korea, you will go through immigration and customs. Most signs are bilingual but knowing Korean phrases speeds up the process.
cult_survival_s4: Using Public Transportation. Korea has one of the best public transit systems in the world. The subway, buses, and KTX (high-speed train) are clean, punctual, and affordable. Get a T-money card for all transit.
cult_survival_s5: Wi-Fi and Phone. Korea has some of the fastest internet in the world. Free Wi-Fi is available almost everywhere. Renting a pocket Wi-Fi or getting a Korean SIM card is easy at the airport.

mstory_340: 새 대표 준혁은 차갑고 엄격한 사람이었습니다. 직원들은 그를 무서워했습니다. 하지만 수진은 당당하게 자신의 의견을 말했습니다.
mstory_346: 준혁은 수진에게 점점 관심을 갖기 시작합니다. 둘은 프로젝트를 함께 하면서 가까워집니다.
mstory_351: 하지만 준혁에게는 비밀이 있었습니다. 그는 사실 회사의 창립자의 아들이었습니다. 이 비밀이 밝혀지면 둘의 관계는 어떻게 될까요?
mstory_357: 한국의 대학 생활은 활기차고 다양합니다. 학생들은 전공 공부뿐만 아니라 동아리 활동도 열심히 합니다.
mstory_362: 중간고사와 기말고사 기간에는 도서관이 항상 가득 찹니다. 밤새 공부하는 학생도 많습니다.
mstory_367: 대학교 축제는 한국 대학의 꽃입니다. 유명한 가수가 와서 공연을 하고 다양한 부스와 행사가 있습니다.
mstory_372: 많은 학생들이 학비를 위해 아르바이트를 합니다. 카페나 편의점에서 일하는 경우가 많습니다.
mstory_376: 취업 준비도 대학 생활의 중요한 부분입니다. 많은 학생들이 인턴십과 자격증 시험을 준비합니다.

aud_found_01_s0_en: Welcome to Korean Foundations. In this first lesson, we are going to learn the ten basic Korean vowels. Korean uses a writing system called Hangul, and vowels are one of its two building blocks.
aud_found_01_s1_en: Let us start with the first vowel. It is written as a vertical line with a short horizontal stroke to the right. This sounds like the A in father. Listen.
aud_found_01_s3_en: The next vowel sounds similar to the U in up or the O in hot. It does not exist in English, so listen carefully.
aud_found_01_s5_en: The next vowel sounds like the O in go. Listen.
aud_found_01_s7_en: This next vowel sounds like the double O in food. Listen.
aud_found_01_s9_en: This next one is tricky. It sounds like the I in the word it, but with your lips spread wide. There is no exact English equivalent. Listen.
aud_found_01_s11_en: The last simple vowel sounds like the double E in see. Listen.
aud_found_01_s13_en: Now the compound vowels. Each one is like a simple vowel but with a Y sound in front. Listen to all four.
aud_found_01_s18_en: Great job. You have learned all ten basic Korean vowels. Six simple vowels and four compound vowels with a Y sound. Practice saying them out loud and you will start hearing them everywhere in Korean.
aud_found_02_s0_en: In this episode, you will learn the most essential Korean greetings and how to introduce yourself. Korean has different levels of politeness, and we will focus on the polite form that you can use with anyone.
aud_found_02_s1_en: The most common greeting in Korean literally means are you at peace. You can use it at any time of day. Listen.
aud_found_02_s3_en: To say goodbye to someone who is leaving, you use a phrase that means go in peace. Listen.
aud_found_02_s5_en: If you are the one leaving, you use a different phrase that means stay in peace. Listen.
aud_found_02_s7_en: To introduce yourself, you say your name followed by a polite ending that means I am. Here are two ways to do it. Listen.
aud_found_02_s10_en: To ask someone their name politely, you use the following phrase. Listen.
aud_found_02_s12_en: After meeting someone, you say a phrase meaning nice to meet you. Listen.
aud_found_02_s14_en: Let us hear a complete introduction dialogue.
aud_found_02_s14_tr: Hello. I am Sujin. What is your name?
aud_found_02_s15_tr: Hello. I am Minho. Nice to meet you.
aud_found_02_s16_en: Excellent work. You now know how to greet people, introduce yourself, and say goodbye in Korean. Remember, Korean has two different goodbyes depending on who is leaving. Practice these phrases and they will become second nature.
aud_found_03_s0_en: Korean has two number systems. Sino-Korean numbers come from Chinese and are used for dates, money, phone numbers, and counting above 100. Native Korean numbers are used for counting things, hours, and ages. Today we will learn both from one to ten.
aud_found_03_s1_en: Let us start with Sino-Korean numbers. These are the ones you will use most often.
aud_found_03_s12_en: Now the native Korean numbers. These may sound completely different, but you will use them when counting objects or telling your age.
aud_found_03_s23_en: Let us practice. How do you say your phone number? You use Sino-Korean numbers. Listen to this phone number.
aud_found_03_s25_en: Well done. You now know both number systems from one to ten. Remember, Sino-Korean for dates, money, and phone numbers. Native Korean for counting things and ages. With practice, choosing the right system will become automatic.
aud_found_04_s0_en: Today we are covering the most essential Korean phrases you need to be polite and get by in any situation. These are your survival phrases for Korea.
aud_found_04_s1_en: Let us begin with thank you. Here is the formal, polite way to say it. Listen.
aud_found_04_s3_en: A slightly more casual but still polite way to say thank you is this. Listen.
aud_found_04_s5_en: To say you are welcome, or it is nothing, use this phrase. Listen.
aud_found_04_s6_tr: You are welcome / It is nothing
aud_found_04_s7_en: To apologize in formal situations, use this phrase. Listen.
aud_found_04_s9_en: For polite but less formal situations, there is a softer version. Listen.
aud_found_04_s11_en: To get someone's attention, there is a word that means excuse me or over here. Listen.
aud_found_04_s13_en: To say I do not understand, use the following. Listen.
aud_found_04_s15_en: To ask someone to speak slowly, use this very useful phrase. Listen.
aud_found_04_s17_en: To ask how much something costs, use this short phrase. Listen.
aud_found_04_s19_en: And to ask where the bathroom is, here is what you say. Listen.
aud_found_04_s21_en: Let us put these together in a real situation. Imagine you are at a Korean market.
aud_found_04_s22_tr: Excuse me, how much is this?
aud_found_04_s25_en: Fantastic. These survival phrases will get you through most basic situations in Korea. The most important ones to memorize are the phrases for hello, thank you, and sorry. Practice them daily and they will become second nature.
aud_daily_01_s0_en: Korea has an incredible cafe culture. In this episode, you will learn how to order drinks and food at a Korean cafe. Let us learn the key phrases.
aud_daily_01_s1_en: When you walk in, the staff will greet you with a warm welcome phrase. Listen.
aud_daily_01_s3_en: To order, you say what you want followed by a word that means please give me. Listen.
aud_daily_01_s5_en: They will ask if you want it hot or iced. Here is how to answer each way. Listen.
aud_daily_01_s8_en: They might ask if you are eating here or taking it to go. Here is how to answer. Listen.
aud_daily_01_s10_tr: I will take it to go
aud_daily_01_s11_en: Let us hear a complete cafe ordering dialogue.
aud_daily_01_s11_tr: Welcome. Would you like to order?
aud_daily_01_s17_en: Perfect. You can now order at a Korean cafe. The key pattern to remember is saying what you want followed by the please give me phrase at the end. Koreans love their coffee culture, so you will use these phrases every day.
aud_daily_02_s0_en: Eating at a Korean restaurant is an experience. In this episode, you will learn how to get a table, order food, and pay the bill. Let us dive in.
aud_daily_02_s1_en: To get a table for two, you simply state the number of people. Listen.
aud_daily_02_s3_en: To ask for the menu, use the please give me pattern you already know. Listen.
aud_daily_02_s5_en: To ask what the staff recommends, use this phrase. Listen.
aud_daily_02_s7_en: To order, you can just point at the menu and say this one please. Listen.
aud_daily_02_s9_en: If you want more side dishes, which are free refills in Korea, here is what to say. Listen.
aud_daily_02_s11_en: To say the food is delicious, use this phrase. It will make the staff very happy. Listen.
aud_daily_02_s13_en: When you are ready to pay, ask for the check using the please give me pattern. Listen.
aud_daily_02_s15_en: In Korea, you usually pay at the counter, not at the table. And tipping is not customary.
aud_daily_02_s16_tr: I ate well (thank you for the meal)
aud_daily_02_s17_en: Excellent. You are now ready to eat at any Korean restaurant. Remember, side dish refills are free, tipping is not expected, and complimenting the food will always bring a smile. There are also traditional phrases said before and after a meal to show gratitude. These are essential Korean dining manners.
aud_daily_03_s0_en: The Seoul subway is one of the best in the world. It is clean, efficient, and easy to use. Let us learn the phrases you need to get around.
aud_daily_03_s1_en: To ask where the subway station is, use this phrase. Listen.
aud_daily_03_s3_en: To ask which line goes to a destination, say the place name and then ask what line number. Listen to an example.
aud_daily_03_s5_en: To ask where to transfer, use this phrase. Listen.
aud_daily_03_s7_en: On the platform, you will hear announcements telling you the next station. Here is what that sounds like. Listen.
aud_daily_03_s8_tr: The next station is Seoul Station
aud_daily_03_s9_en: To ask someone for help, you can politely ask if you are going the right direction. Listen.
aud_daily_03_s10_tr: Excuse me, is this the right direction?
aud_daily_03_s12_en: Great job. The Seoul subway is very foreigner friendly with announcements in Korean, English, Chinese, and Japanese. But knowing these Korean phrases will make your commute smoother and impress the locals.
aud_gram_01_s0_en: Korean sentence structure is different from English. In English, we say subject verb object. I eat rice. In Korean, the verb always comes at the end. So it becomes I rice eat. Let us practice this pattern.
aud_gram_01_s1_en: In English we say I eat rice. In Korean, the order becomes I, rice, eat. The verb always goes at the end. Listen.
aud_gram_01_s3_en: Let us try another. I drink water follows the same pattern. I, water, drink. Listen.
aud_gram_01_s5_en: Notice the verb always comes last. This is the golden rule of Korean grammar. No matter how long the sentence is, the verb goes at the end.
aud_gram_01_s8_en: Now you try. If I want to say I read a book, what comes last? The verb, read. The pattern is I, book, read. Listen.
aud_gram_01_s10_en: Remember, the verb always goes at the end in Korean. Subject, object, verb. Once you internalize this pattern, Korean grammar becomes much more logical. Everything else just slots in before the verb.
aud_gram_02_s0_en: Particles are small words attached to nouns that show their role in a sentence. They are one of the most important parts of Korean grammar. Today we will learn the four most essential ones.
aud_gram_02_s1_en: First, the topic markers. These come in two forms depending on whether the preceding word ends in a consonant or a vowel. They mark what the sentence is about. Listen.
aud_gram_02_s2_tr: I am a student (as for me)
aud_gram_02_s4_en: Next, the subject markers. These also come in two forms depending on the final sound. They mark who or what performs the action. Listen.
aud_gram_02_s7_en: The object markers show what receives the action. Again, there are two forms depending on the final sound of the noun. Listen.
aud_gram_02_s10_en: Finally, the location particle marks where something is or where you are going. Listen.
aud_gram_02_s13_en: To summarize, you learned four types of particles. The topic marker, the subject marker, the object marker, and the location marker. Each has two forms depending on the final sound of the word before it. These four particle types are the backbone of Korean grammar. Once you master them, everything else falls into place.
aud_gram_03_s0_en: Making past tense in Korean is actually quite regular and predictable. You take the verb stem and add the past tense marker, then the polite ending. Let us see how this works.
aud_gram_03_s1_en: The past tense marker changes slightly depending on the vowel in the verb stem. Let us start with the verb to eat. Listen to the present form, then the past form.
aud_gram_03_s4_en: Now the verb to go. Listen to present and then past.
aud_gram_03_s7_en: Now the verb to do, which is also used for to study. Listen.
aud_gram_03_s10_en: And the verb to drink. Listen.
aud_gram_03_s13_en: Let us practice with full sentences. Yesterday I went to school.
aud_gram_03_s16_en: Great work. The past tense pattern is consistent. Just add the past tense marker to the verb stem. The marker changes slightly depending on the vowel in the stem, but with practice this becomes automatic.
aud_cult_01_s0_en: Korean has multiple speech levels that change based on who you are talking to. This is not just about being polite. It is built into the grammar itself. Let us understand the three most important levels.
aud_cult_01_s1_en: The most formal level is used in business, speeches, news broadcasts, and with strangers who are clearly older. You will recognize it by its distinctive ending. Listen.
aud_cult_01_s4_en: The polite level is the most versatile. You can use it with almost anyone and in almost any situation. You will notice it always ends with a short syllable. Listen.
aud_cult_01_s7_en: The casual level drops that polite ending entirely. Only use this with close friends your age or younger, children, or people who have specifically said it is okay. Listen.
aud_cult_01_s10_en: Using the wrong speech level can be very awkward. If in doubt, always use the polite form. It is safe in almost every situation.
aud_cult_01_s11_en: Let us compare all three levels with one phrase. I am going.
aud_cult_01_s15_en: To summarize, use the formal level in business or official settings. Use the polite level for everyday situations and anyone you do not know well. Only use casual speech with close friends your age. When in doubt, the polite form is always safe.
aud_cult_02_s0_en: In Korean culture, age is extremely important. It determines how you speak to someone, how you pour drinks, and even who eats first. Let us understand this system.
aud_cult_02_s1_en: When Koreans meet someone new, one of the first questions is often what year were you born. This is not rude. It helps both people know how to address each other.
aud_cult_02_s3_en: If someone is older, there are special titles you use that mean older brother and older sister. The words are different depending on whether you are male or female. These are used widely beyond family. Listen.
aud_cult_02_s8_en: When pouring drinks for someone older, use two hands. When receiving a drink from someone older, also use two hands or support one hand with the other.
aud_cult_02_s9_en: At meals, the oldest person picks up their chopsticks first. It is polite to wait.
aud_cult_02_s11_en: Understanding age hierarchy is essential for navigating Korean social situations. It is not about being less than someone older. It is about showing respect, and Koreans deeply appreciate when foreigners understand this system.
aud_travel_01_s0_en: You have landed at Incheon International Airport. Let us learn the phrases you will need to get through immigration, find your luggage, and get to your destination.
aud_travel_01_s1_en: At immigration, the officer might ask the purpose of your visit. Here is how to say sightseeing, and how to say travel. Listen.
aud_travel_01_s4_en: They may ask how long you are staying. You can answer in days or weeks. Listen.
aud_travel_01_s7_en: To find your luggage carousel, look for the baggage claim signs or ask. Listen.
aud_travel_01_s9_en: To find the airport express train to Seoul, use this phrase. Listen.
aud_travel_01_s10_tr: Where do I catch the airport train?
aud_travel_01_s11_en: To get a taxi, head to the taxi stand and say your destination followed by a phrase meaning please take me there. Listen.
aud_travel_01_s12_tr: Please take me to Seoul Station
aud_travel_01_s13_en: You are ready for Incheon Airport. Remember, the airport has great English signage, but using Korean will make everything smoother and the staff will appreciate the effort. For taxis, just remember your destination followed by the please take me there phrase.
aud_travel_02_s0_en: Whether you are at a luxury hotel or a cozy guesthouse, these phrases will help you check in and handle common situations during your stay.
aud_travel_02_s1_en: When you arrive, tell the front desk you have a reservation. Here is how. Listen.
aud_travel_02_s3_en: They will ask for your name. You can spell it out or show your booking confirmation on your phone.
aud_travel_02_s4_tr: I would like to check in
aud_travel_02_s5_en: To ask about the wifi password, use the following phrase. Listen.
aud_travel_02_s7_en: If you need extra towels, use the please give me pattern you already know. Listen.
aud_travel_02_s9_en: To ask what time checkout is, use this phrase. Listen.
aud_travel_02_s12_en: Most Korean hotels have staff who speak some English, but using Korean shows respect and often gets you better service. The most useful pattern is the please give me phrase at the end of any request. It works everywhere.
aud_travel_03_s0_en: Nobody wants an emergency on vacation, but being prepared with the right Korean phrases can make a huge difference. Let us cover the most important ones.
aud_travel_03_s1_en: The most important phrase to know is how to ask for help. Listen carefully.
aud_travel_03_s3_en: If you need to call emergency services, the number in Korea is 119 for fire and ambulance, and 112 for police. Here is how to say you need an ambulance.
aud_travel_03_s5_en: If you are feeling sick, you can describe it simply. Here is how to say you are not feeling well. Listen.
aud_travel_03_s6_tr: I am sick, or it hurts
aud_travel_03_s7_en: To say where it hurts, you put the body part before this word. For example, to say my stomach hurts. Listen.
aud_travel_03_s10_en: To find a pharmacy, which in Korea is called a yakguk. Listen.
aud_travel_03_s14_en: If you have lost something, here is how to explain. Listen.
aud_travel_03_s17_en: And to tell someone you do not speak Korean well. Listen.
aud_travel_03_s19_ko: 영어를 할 수 있는 분 있어요?
aud_travel_03_s19_tr: Is there anyone who speaks English?
aud_travel_03_s20_en: Let us practice a quick scenario. You are at a pharmacy explaining you have a cold.
aud_travel_03_s23_en: These emergency phrases could be a lifesaver. The pattern for where is something uses the location question ending. And for saying something hurts, just put the body part before the pain word. Write these down and keep them accessible during your trip.
aud_real_01_s0_en: Making Korean friends is one of the best ways to learn the language. In this episode, we will break down a real conversation between two people meeting for the first time at a cafe. The key is knowing the right age-related questions, because age determines how you speak to each other in Korean.
aud_real_01_s1_en: When Koreans meet someone new, one of the first things they want to know is your age. This is not rude in Korean culture. It determines the level of politeness. Here is how they ask. Listen.
aud_real_01_s2_tr: How old are you? casual polite
aud_real_01_s3_en: A more polite version for someone who might be older. Listen.
aud_real_01_s4_tr: May I ask your age? formal
aud_real_01_s5_en: When you find out you are the same age, Koreans get very excited. They use a special word for someone born in the same year. Listen.
aud_real_01_s7_en: If you are the same age or close in age, they might suggest dropping the formalities and speaking casually. This is a big moment in Korean friendships. Listen.
aud_real_01_s10_en: Now let us hear a natural conversation between two people meeting at a cafe meetup.
aud_real_01_s11_tr: Hello! Is this the Korean study meetup?
aud_real_01_s12_ko: 네, 맞아요! 저는 수진이에요. 이름이 뭐예요?
aud_real_01_s12_tr: Yes, that is right! I am Sujin. What is your name?
aud_real_01_s13_ko: 저는 마이클이에요. 한국어 공부한 지 6개월 됐어요.
aud_real_01_s13_tr: I am Michael. I have been studying Korean for 6 months.
aud_real_01_s14_ko: 와, 한국어 잘하시네요! 혹시 나이가 어떻게 되세요?
aud_real_01_s14_tr: Wow, your Korean is good! May I ask how old you are?
aud_real_01_s16_tr: Me too! Same age! Then let us speak casually.
aud_real_01_s17_en: Notice how naturally the conversation shifted from formal to casual once they discovered they were the same age. This is very typical in Korean social interactions.
aud_real_01_s18_en: Here are a few useful follow-up phrases for when you are getting to know someone. Listen.
aud_real_01_s20_tr: Can you give me your KakaoTalk ID?
aud_real_01_s21_tr: Let us meet again next time
aud_real_01_s22_en: The age question is the gateway to Korean friendships. Once you know each other is age, the relationship has a clear structure. Same age friends are called chingu, and this bond is actually very meaningful in Korean culture. Practice these phrases and you will make Korean friends in no time.
aud_real_02_s0_en: Korean texting is a whole different language. Koreans use abbreviations, special characters, and expressions that you will never find in a textbook. After this episode, you will be able to text like a Korean.
aud_real_02_s1_en: The most common text expression you will see is the Korean laughing sound. It looks like a series of the consonant ki-euk repeated. Listen.
aud_real_02_s3_en: A softer laugh, more like a giggle, uses the consonant hi-eut. Listen.
aud_real_02_s5_en: When Koreans are sad or crying in text, they use the consonant that sounds like a cry. Listen.
aud_real_02_s6_tr: crying face, like tears rolling down
aud_real_02_s7_en: A similar crying expression using a different character. Listen.
aud_real_02_s9_en: Now for common abbreviations. Koreans love shortening phrases. Here is how they shorten the phrase for I understand or OK. Listen to the full phrase first.
aud_real_02_s13_en: Another very common text abbreviation means really or for real. The full word is.
aud_real_02_s15_en: In texts this often becomes just the first consonants.
aud_real_02_s17_en: Here is a common text conversation between friends making plans.
aud_real_02_s24_en: Korean texting is all about speed and brevity. The consonant-only abbreviations might look strange at first, but once you get used to them, you will feel like a real insider. Start using these in your Korean chats and your Korean friends will be impressed.
aud_real_03_s0_en: If you watch K-dramas, you have probably heard certain phrases over and over. In this episode, we will break down the most iconic K-drama phrases and tell you which ones you can actually use in real life.
aud_real_03_s1_en: Let us start with the classic dramatic confession. In every romance drama, you will hear this. Listen.

aud_real_03_s5_en: These are actually used in real life too, just not as dramatically. Now here is the classic drama breakup phrase. Listen.
aud_real_03_s7_en: The dramatic wrist grab scene always comes with this phrase. Listen.
aud_real_03_s9_en: Now for some fun ones. The classic surprised reaction. Listen.
aud_real_03_s11_en: You can absolutely use this one in everyday conversation. Another one you hear constantly in dramas when someone does something wrong. Listen.
aud_real_03_s13_en: Be careful with this one. It is very casual and can sound rude if used with the wrong person. Between close friends it is fine.
aud_real_03_s14_en: The classic chaebol drama phrase when someone is being dramatic about their identity. Listen.
aud_real_03_s15_tr: Who do you think I am?
aud_real_03_s16_en: And the wholesome one. When characters are eating together and the food is amazing. Listen.
aud_real_03_s18_en: Here is one more you hear in almost every drama. The fighting or you can do it cheer. Listen.
aud_real_03_s20_en: This one is used everywhere in real Korean life. At work, before exams, at sports games. It is borrowed from English but has become completely Korean.
aud_real_03_s21_en: Now try saying each phrase out loud after you hear it. Ready?
aud_real_03_s25_en: K-dramas are actually a fantastic way to learn Korean because you hear natural emotional speech. The phrases we covered today range from usable every day to drama only. The safe ones for real life are really, I like you, this is delicious, and fighting. Save the dramatic ones for joking around with close Korean friends.
aud_deep_01_s0_en: Korean speech levels are one of the hardest things for foreigners to master. Getting them wrong can make you sound rude or awkwardly formal. In this deep dive, we will break down exactly when to use each level so you never have to guess.
aud_deep_01_s1_en: Korean has three main speech levels you need to know. The first is formal polite, used in business, news, and with strangers much older than you. The ending sounds like this. Listen to the verb to eat in formal polite.
aud_deep_01_s3_en: The second level is informal polite. This is your default. Use it with most people. It ends with the yo sound. Listen.
aud_deep_01_s5_en: The third level is casual, called banmal. Only use this with close friends your age or younger, children, or animals. Listen.
aud_deep_01_s7_en: Here is the golden rule. When in doubt, use informal polite with the yo ending. You can never go wrong with it. Let us hear the same sentence in all three levels. I am going to school.
aud_deep_01_s8_tr: I go to school, formal polite
aud_deep_01_s9_tr: I go to school, informal polite
aud_deep_01_s11_en: Now let us talk about honorific vocabulary. Some words completely change depending on who you are talking about. For example, the word to eat.
aud_deep_01_s14_en: So if you are asking an older person if they have eaten, you would say. Listen.
aud_deep_01_s16_en: The word for sleep also changes.
aud_deep_01_s19_en: And the word for house or home.
aud_deep_01_s22_en: Let us practice choosing the right level. Your Korean friend is the same age as you and you have been friends for a year. Which level do you use?
aud_deep_01_s23_en: You would use casual, banmal. But if you meet their parents, you immediately switch to formal polite. Let us hear both.
aud_deep_01_s24_tr: Hey, did you eat? to friend
aud_deep_01_s25_tr: Hello, mother. Have you eaten? to friend is parent
aud_deep_01_s26_en: The key takeaway is this. Default to informal polite with the yo ending. Only drop to casual when someone your age explicitly says it is okay. Always use formal polite or honorific vocabulary with elders, bosses, and strangers. Getting this right is the single fastest way to sound natural in Korean.
aud_deep_02_s0_en: In English, you say three apples or two books. In Korean, you need a special counter word between the number and the noun. Think of it like saying three pieces of paper or two cups of coffee. Korean does this for everything.
aud_deep_02_s1_en: The most common counter is the general counter for things. It uses native Korean numbers. Listen.
aud_deep_02_s5_en: But when you attach a counter, the numbers one through four change their form slightly. One, hana, becomes han. Two, dul, becomes du. Three, set, becomes se. Four, net, becomes ne. Listen to counting apples.
aud_deep_02_s9_en: For people, you use a different counter. Listen.
aud_deep_02_s13_en: For bottles of drinks, there is another counter. Listen.
aud_deep_02_s15_en: For cups, like ordering coffee. Listen.
aud_deep_02_s21_en: Here is a useful restaurant scenario. You need a table for two people and want to order three bottles of soju.
aud_deep_02_s22_tr: Two people, meaning party of two
aud_deep_02_s24_en: Do not try to memorize every counter. Start with these five: the general counter for things, the counter for people, bottles, cups, and animals. Together they cover about 80 percent of daily situations. When in doubt, use the general counter and Koreans will understand you perfectly.
